chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly provides working links to the Mutiny framework and scripts in Reference [24] (https://dessert.unina.it:8088/marcobarlo/mutiny). However, these are hosted on a university server with an HTTP port exposed, which may not constitute a permanent, public repository like GitHub/GitLab accessible without specific institutional access or potential expiration. While the code is available, it lacks the robustness of a standard open-source repository link.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation was conducted in an on-premises cluster consisting of 1 control plane node and 4 worker nodes running as Virtual Machines (VMs) on top of VirtualBox 6 hypervisor. This is a small-scale simulation environment, not a physical testbed with real hardware or a massive-scale cloud deployment.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper contains a dedicated section (Section IV-A) that formally defines the fault model. It explicitly details three types of faults: bit-flips, data-type corruption (value set), and message drops. It also specifies assumptions about the asynchronous nature of state changes and the specific components targeted (Apiserver to Etcd).
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides a rigorous, data-driven analysis of trade-offs. It quantifies the exact percentage of injections leading to various failure modes (e.g., 3% system-wide failures, 24% under/over provisioning) and analyzes overhead in terms of latency (z-scores), resource exhaustion, and control plane load. It also discusses the cost of misconfigurations vs. resilience strategies.

major_weakness: The experimental setup relies on a small-scale simulation using VirtualBox VMs rather than physical hardware or large-scale cloud deployments, limiting the generalizability of the results to massive production clusters.
